# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]  
**Define an analytic function. State the Cauchy–Riemann equations in Cartesian form.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A function $f(z)$ is said to be **analytic** at a point $z_0$ if it is complex differentiable at $z_0$ *and* at every point in some neighbourhood of $z_0$. A function is analytic in a region $R$ if it is analytic at every point of $R$.
> 
> Writing $f(z) = u(x, y) + i\,v(x, y)$, the **Cauchy–Riemann equations** in Cartesian form are:
> 
> $$u_x = v_y, \qquad u_y = -v_x$$
> 
> These are necessary for differentiability; they become sufficient when the partial derivatives $u_x, u_y, v_x, v_y$ are continuous in a neighbourhood of the point.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]  
**What are harmonic conjugates? Why is it relevant in life-science modelling?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Two real-valued functions $u(x, y)$ and $v(x, y)$ are called **harmonic conjugates** if $f(z) = u + iv$ is analytic, equivalently:
> 
> - $u$ and $v$ both satisfy Laplace's equation $u_{xx} + u_{yy} = 0$ and $v_{xx} + v_{yy} = 0$,
> - they satisfy the C–R equations $u_x = v_y$, $u_y = -v_x$.
> 
> **Life-science relevance:** Harmonic functions model **steady-state diffusion** of oxygen, drugs, or heat in planar biological tissue. They guarantee *no accumulation* and *no source* inside the domain — a fundamental physical conservation law in steady biological processes.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** If $f(z) = u(x, y) + i\,v(x, y)$ is analytic and $u = e^x \cos y$, find the analytic function $f(z)$ and hence evaluate $f'(z)$.  
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Show that $u = e^{-x}(x \cos y + y \sin y)$ is harmonic. Find its harmonic conjugate $v$ and form $f(z)$.  
**(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) — Finding $f(z)$ from $u = e^x \cos y$:**

**Step 1 — Verify $u$ is harmonic (sanity check):**

$$u_x = e^x \cos y,\;\; u_{xx} = e^x \cos y$$
$$u_y = -e^x \sin y,\;\; u_{yy} = -e^x \cos y$$
$$u_{xx} + u_{yy} = e^x \cos y - e^x \cos y = 0 \;\checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Use C–R to find $v_y$ and $v_x$:**

$$v_y = u_x = e^x \cos y$$
$$v_x = -u_y = e^x \sin y$$

**Step 3 — Integrate $v_y$ with respect to $y$:**

$$v(x, y) = \int e^x \cos y\,dy = e^x \sin y + g(x)$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate this $v$ with respect to $x$ and match to $v_x$:**

$$v_x = e^x \sin y + g'(x) = e^x \sin y \;\Rightarrow\; g'(x) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; g(x) = C$$

**Step 5 — Assemble $f(z)$ (taking $C = 0$):**

$$f(z) = e^x \cos y + i\,e^x \sin y = e^{x+iy} = e^z$$

**Step 6 — Compute $f'(z)$:**

$$f'(z) = u_x + i\,v_x = e^x \cos y + i\,e^x \sin y = e^x(\cos y + i\sin y) = e^z \;\;\checkmark$$

> **Mark split (a):** Harmonic verification — 2, C–R setup — 2, Integration of $v$ — 2, $f'(z)$ — 1.

**(b) — $u = e^{-x}(x \cos y + y \sin y)$:**

**Step 1 — Compute the partials of $u$:**

$$u_x = -e^{-x}(x\cos y + y\sin y) + e^{-x}\cos y = e^{-x}\bigl[(1-x)\cos y - y\sin y\bigr]$$

$$u_y = e^{-x}\bigl[-x\sin y + \sin y + y\cos y\bigr] = e^{-x}\bigl[(1-x)\sin y + y\cos y\bigr]$$

$$u_{xx} = -e^{-x}\bigl[(1-x)\cos y - y\sin y\bigr] + e^{-x}\bigl[-\cos y\bigr] = e^{-x}\bigl[(x-2)\cos y + y\sin y\bigr]$$

$$u_{yy} = e^{-x}\bigl[-(1-x)\cos y - y\sin y + \cos y\bigr] = e^{-x}\bigl[x\cos y - y\sin y\bigr]$$

**Step 2 — Verify harmonicity:**

$$u_{xx} + u_{yy} = e^{-x}\bigl[(x-2)\cos y + y\sin y + x\cos y - y\sin y\bigr] = e^{-x}\bigl[(2x-2)\cos y\bigr]$$

Wait — let us recompute carefully:

$$u_{xx} = e^{-x}(x-2)\cos y + e^{-x}\,y\sin y$$
$$u_{yy} = -e^{-x}(1-x)\sin y - e^{-x}\,y\sin y + e^{-x}\cos y = e^{-x}x\cos y - e^{-x}\,y\sin y$$

Adding:

$$u_{xx} + u_{yy} = e^{-x}[(x-2)\cos y + y\sin y + x\cos y - y\sin y] = e^{-x}(2x-2)\cos y$$

This is **not identically zero**! Let me recheck the problem — the standard textbook problem is $u = e^{-x}(x \cos y + y \sin y)$ and it IS harmonic. The expected $u_{xx} + u_{yy} = 0$ holds; the partial expansions above are correct, so let me recheck:

$$u_{xx} = e^{-x}(x-2)\cos y + e^{-x}\,y\sin y$$
$$u_{yy} = e^{-x}x\cos y - e^{-x}\,y\sin y$$

$$u_{xx} + u_{yy} = e^{-x}\bigl[(x-2)\cos y + y\sin y + x\cos y - y\sin y\bigr] = e^{-x}(2x - 2)\cos y$$

This is **not zero** — so this candidate $u$ is **not harmonic**. The standard problem uses $u = e^x(x \cos y - y \sin y)$ instead. Let us present the *corrected* standard KTU problem.

**Corrected (b) — $u = e^x(x \cos y - y \sin y)$:**

**Step 1 — Partial derivatives:**

$$u_x = e^x(x\cos y - y\sin y) + e^x\cos y = e^x\bigl[(x+1)\cos y - y\sin y\bigr]$$

$$u_{xx} = e^x\bigl[(x+2)\cos y - y\sin y\bigr]$$

$$u_y = e^x\bigl[-x\sin y - \sin y - y\cos y\bigr] = -e^x\bigl[(x+1)\sin y + y\cos y\bigr]$$

$$u_{yy} = -e^x\bigl[(x+1)\cos y + \cos y - y\sin y\bigr] = -e^x\bigl[(x+2)\cos y - y\sin y\bigr]$$

**Step 2 — Verify harmonicity:**

$$u_{xx} + u_{yy} = e^x[(x+2)\cos y - y\sin y] - e^x[(x+2)\cos y - y\sin y] = 0 \;\checkmark$$

**Step 3 — Find $v$ via C–R:**

$$v_y = u_x = e^x\bigl[(x+1)\cos y - y\sin y\bigr]$$
$$v = \int v_y\,dy = e^x\bigl[(x+1)\sin y + y\cos y - \sin y\bigr] + g(x) = e^x\bigl[x\sin y + y\cos y\bigr] + g(x)$$

**Step 4 — Match $v_x$:**

$$v_x = e^x[x\sin y + y\cos y] + g'(x) \quad\stackrel{!}{=}\quad -u_y = e^x[(x+1)\sin y + y\cos y]$$

Comparing: $e^x x\sin y + g'(x) = e^x(x+1)\sin y \;\Rightarrow\; g'(x) = e^x \sin y$... 

This has a $y$-dependence which is impossible. Recheck — the standard answer is $v = e^x(y\cos y + x\sin y) + C$ and $f(z) = ze^z$. Let me redo with $u = e^x(x \cos y - y \sin y)$ carefully:

$$u_y = e^x(-x\sin y - \sin y - y\cos y) = -e^x[(x+1)\sin y + y\cos y]$$

So $v_x = -u_y = e^x[(x+1)\sin y + y\cos y]$.

Integrating $v_x$ w.r.t. $x$: $v = e^x[(x+1)\sin y + y\cos y] - e^x\sin y + h(y) = e^x[x\sin y + y\cos y] + h(y)$.

Differentiate w.r.t. $y$: $v_y = e^x[x\cos y + \cos y - y\sin y] + h'(y) = e^x[(x+1)\cos y - y\sin y] + h'(y)$.

But $v_y$ must equal $u_x = e^x[(x+1)\cos y - y\sin y]$, so $h'(y) = 0$, $h(y) = C$.

**Step 5 — Final answer:**

$$\boxed{f(z) = u + iv = e^x(x\cos y - y\sin y) + i\,e^x(x\sin y + y\cos y) = (x+iy)\,e^{x+iy} = z\,e^z}$$

$$f'(z) = e^z + z\,e^z = (1 + z)\,e^z$$

> **Mark split (b):** $u_{xx}, u_{yy}$ — 2, Harmonicity — 1, C–R & integration — 3, Final $f(z)$ — 1.

### Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Prove that $f(z) = \bar z$ is not analytic at any point in $\mathbb{C}$.  
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** State and prove the Cauchy–Riemann equations in polar form. Hence show that $f(z) = \log z$ is analytic for $r > 0$.  
**(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) — $f(z) = \bar z = x - iy$ is nowhere analytic:**

Here $u(x, y) = x$, $v(x, y) = -y$. Compute partials:

$$u_x = 1,\quad u_y = 0,\quad v_x = 0,\quad v_y = -1$$

**Test C–R:**

$$u_x = 1 \neq -1 = v_y \quad \text{(fails)}$$

$$u_y = 0 = -v_x = 0 \quad \text{(holds)}$$

Since the first C–R equation fails everywhere, $f(z) = \bar z$ is **not analytic at any point** of $\mathbb{C}$. $\blacksquare$

> **Alternative direct proof via the limit:** Take $\Delta z$ along real axis: $f'(z) = 1$. Take $\Delta z$ along imaginary axis: $f'(z) = -1$. Contradiction, hence the derivative does not exist.

> **Mark split (a):** Setting up $u, v$ — 2, Partial derivatives — 2, C–R test — 2, Conclusion — 1.

**(b) — Polar C–R derivation:**

**Statement:** If $f(z) = u(r, \theta) + i\,v(r, \theta)$ is analytic at $z = r e^{i\theta}$ (with $z \neq 0$), then:

$$r\,u_r = v_\theta \quad\text{and}\quad r\,v_r = -u_\theta$$

**Proof:** Let $w = \log z = \log r + i\theta$. The function $g(w) = f(e^w)$ is an analytic function of $w = \alpha + i\beta$ where $\alpha = \log r$, $\beta = \theta$. Applying the Cartesian C–R to $g$:

$$U_\alpha = V_\beta, \quad U_\beta = -V_\alpha$$

where $U(\alpha, \beta) = u(e^\alpha \cos\beta,\, e^\alpha \sin\beta)$ etc. By the chain rule:

$$U_\alpha = u_x \cdot e^\alpha \cos\beta + u_y \cdot e^\alpha \sin\beta = r(u_x \cos\theta + u_y \sin\theta)$$
$$U_\beta = -u_x r\sin\theta + u_y r\cos\theta = r(-u_x\sin\theta + u_y\cos\theta)$$

And $V_\alpha, V_\beta$ similarly with $v$. Substituting $U_\alpha = V_\beta$:

$$u_x \cos\theta + u_y \sin\theta = -v_x \sin\theta + v_y \cos\theta$$

Equating coefficients of $\cos\theta$ and $\sin\theta$ and using Cartesian C–R yields the polar form. $\blacksquare$

**Application to $f(z) = \log z$:**

With $u = \log r$, $v = \theta$:

$$r\,u_r = r \cdot \frac{1}{r} = 1 = v_\theta \;\checkmark$$
$$r\,v_r = 0 = -u_\theta \;\checkmark$$

Both polar C–R equations hold for $r > 0$, so $f(z) = \log z$ is analytic in the punctured plane $\mathbb{C} \setminus \{0\}$ (excluding the branch cut on the negative real axis for the principal branch). $\blacksquare$

> **Mark split (b):** Statement — 1, Setup — 2, Chain rule computation — 2, Polar C–R derivation — 1, Application to $\log z$ — 1.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 1 — Analytic Functions:**
> 
> 1. **Conflating "differentiable" with "analytic"** — A function can be differentiable at one point but not analytic. Always mention the *neighbourhood* condition for analyticity. **Loss: up to 2 marks.**
> 
> 2. **Forgetting to verify continuity of partials** — Sufficiency of C–R requires continuity of $u_x, u_y, v_x, v_y$ in a neighbourhood. Many students quote C–R alone. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
> 
> 3. **Sign error in the second C–R equation** — The correct form is $u_y = -v_x$, **not** $u_y = v_x$. Memorise the pairing: $u_x \leftrightarrow +v_y$ and $u_y \leftrightarrow -v_x$. **Loss: 1 mark per occurrence.**
> 
> 4. **Not stating the region of analyticity** — KTU expects you to write "analytic in $\mathbb{C}$", "analytic in $\mathbb{C} \setminus \{0\}$", or similar. Just computing $f'(z)$ is incomplete. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 
> 5. **Using $f'(z) = -u_y + iv_x$ instead of $u_x + iv_x$** — The derivative has a unique value; pick the formula based on which partials you already computed. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 
> 6. **Ignoring branch cuts for $\log z$ and $z^{1/2}$** — These functions are not analytic on the negative real axis for the principal branch. Always mention the cut. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 — Analytic Function: Rapid-Revision Checklist**
> 
> - **Differentiability** = existence of $\displaystyle \lim_{\Delta z \to 0} \frac{f(z+\Delta z) - f(z)}{\Delta z}$ (path-independent).
> - **Analyticity** = differentiability in a *neighbourhood* of the point.
> - **C–R (Cartesian):** $u_x = v_y$, $u_y = -v_x$.
> - **C–R (Polar):** $r u_r = v_\theta$, $r v_r = -u_\theta$.
> - **Derivative:** $f'(z) = u_x + i v_x = v_y - i u_y = e^{-i\theta}(u_r + i v_r)$.
> - **Harmonic:** $u_{xx} + u_{yy} = 0$ and $v_{xx} + v_{yy} = 0$.
> - **Sufficiency:** C–R *plus* continuous partials in a neighbourhood ⇒ differentiable.
> - **Necessity:** Differentiable ⇒ C–R holds.
> - **Counter-examples:** $\bar z$ is nowhere analytic; $\vert z \vert^2$ differentiable only at $z = 0$.
> - **Milne–Thomson method:** Given $u$ (or $v$) harmonic, find its conjugate $v$ (or $u$) using C–R.
> - **Standard analytic functions:** polynomials, $e^z, \sin z, \cos z, \sinh z, \cosh z, \log z$ (away from branch cut).
> - **Life-science link:** Steady-state 2D diffusion, conformal mapping for biological shapes, electrocardiology potentials.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cauchy Riemann Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDMAT201 - MATHEMATICS FOR LIFE SCIENCE - 2
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cauchy Riemann Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1: COMPLEX ANALYSIS — CAUCHY RIEMANN EQUATIONS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cauchy-Riemann Equations (CR Equations):** If $f(z) = u(x, y) + i\,v(x, y)$ is a complex-valued function of a complex variable $z = x + iy$, where $u, v$ are real-valued functions of real variables $x$ and $y$, then $f(z)$ is **differentiable at a point** $z_0$ if and only if the partial derivatives $u_x, u_y, v_x, v_y$ exist at $z_0$ and satisfy the following pair of first-order partial differential equations:

$$
\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = -\frac{\partial v}{\partial x}
$$

These two simultaneous relations are universally known as the **Cauchy–Riemann (CR) Equations**, named after the French mathematician **Augustin-Louis Cauchy** (1789–1857) and the German mathematician **Georg Friedrich Bernhard Riemann** (1826–1866).

> [!NOTE]
> **Sufficiency Condition:** If $u_x, u_y, v_x, v_y$ exist, are **continuous** in a neighbourhood of $z_0$, and satisfy the CR equations at $z_0$, then $f(z)$ is **analytic (holomorphic)** at $z_0$. Without continuity of the partials, CR equations alone are *necessary but not sufficient*.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "GPS Coordinates of a Smooth Surface"

Imagine you are standing on a perfectly smooth, polished marble surface (a representation of the complex plane $\mathbb{C}$). Now, you have a weather map drawn on that surface showing the **real part** (say, temperature $u$) and the **imaginary part** (say, pressure $v$) at every point.

For the surface to be physically "smooth" (i.e., for the function to be **differentiable** at every point), the temperature gradient and pressure gradient must be perfectly **synchronised** in two specific directions:
- East–West: the rate of change of temperature must equal the rate of change of pressure in the North–South direction.
- North–South: the rate of change of temperature must equal the **negative** of the rate of change of pressure in the East–West direction.

If this synchronisation breaks at any point, the surface develops a "kink" (a non-differentiable point) — analogous to a function failing to be analytic.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** *"u rides the x-track with v on the y-track. u dips the y-track, v dips the x-track — but in the opposite direction."*
> - $u_x = +v_y$ (same sign, cross-matched)
> - $u_y = -v_x$ (opposite sign, cross-matched)

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard Differentiation Symbol:** $\displaystyle f'(z) = \lim_{\Delta z \to 0}\frac{f(z + \Delta z) - f(z)}{\Delta z}$
- **Domain of Differentiability:** Open, connected subset of $\mathbb{C}$ (denoted $D$)
- **Associated Identity:** $f'(z) = u_x + i\,v_x = v_y - i\,u_y$ (used in KTU problems to compute derivatives once CR holds)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Necessity of CR Equations — A Structured Logical Breakdown

The derivation hinges on a single foundational idea: **the limit defining $f'(z)$ must be path-independent**, since $\Delta z \to 0$ in $\mathbb{C}$ can occur along infinitely many directions.

### Step 1 — Write the difference quotient
Let $z = x + iy$ and $\Delta z = \Delta x + i\,\Delta y$. The increment of $f$ is:

$$
\Delta f = f(z + \Delta z) - f(z) = \Delta u + i\,\Delta v
$$

### Step 2 — Form the limit
$$
f'(z) = \lim_{\Delta z \to 0} \frac{\Delta u + i\,\Delta v}{\Delta x + i\,\Delta y}
$$

### Step 3 — Approach along the **real axis** ($\Delta y = 0$)
Setting $\Delta y = 0$, the limit reduces to a pure real-variable limit:

$$
f'(z) = \lim_{\Delta x \to 0}\left(\frac{\Delta u}{\Delta x} + i\,\frac{\Delta v}{\Delta x}\right) = u_x + i\,v_x \qquad \text{\textbf{...(1)}}
$$

### Step 4 — Approach along the **imaginary axis** ($\Delta x = 0$)
Setting $\Delta x = 0$, $\Delta z = i\,\Delta y$:

$$
f'(z) = \lim_{\Delta y \to 0}\frac{\Delta u + i\,\Delta v}{i\,\Delta y}
$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $-i$:

$$
f'(z) = \lim_{\Delta y \to 0}\left(-i\,\frac{\Delta u}{\Delta y} + \frac{\Delta v}{\Delta y}\right) = -i\,u_y + v_y \qquad \text{\textbf{...(2)}}
$$

### Step 5 — Equate the two expressions
Equating real and imaginary parts of **(1)** and **(2)**:

$$
\boxed{u_x = v_y} \quad \text{and} \quad \boxed{v_x = -u_y}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The equations $u_x = v_y$ and $u_y = -v_x$ together are the **Cauchy–Riemann equations in Cartesian form**.

### Step 2.2 — The Converse (Sufficiency)

If $u, v$ have **continuous** first-order partial derivatives in a neighbourhood of $z_0$ and satisfy the CR equations at $z_0$, then $f$ is differentiable at $z_0$. This is the theorem that promotes "CR + continuity" to "differentiability."

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Identity / Formula | Form | Conditions |
|:-:|:--|:--|:--|
| 1 | $u_x = v_y$ | CR Equation 1 | $u, v$ real-differentiable |
| 2 | $u_y = -v_x$ | CR Equation 2 | $u, v$ real-differentiable |
| 3 | $f'(z) = u_x + i\,v_x$ | Derivative via $x$ | After CR is satisfied |
| 4 | $f'(z) = v_y - i\,u_y$ | Derivative via $y$ | After CR is satisfied |
| 5 | $\Delta u = u_x\,\Delta x + u_y\,\Delta y$ | Total increment of $u$ | First-order Taylor |
| 6 | $\Delta v = v_x\,\Delta x + v_y\,\Delta y$ | Total increment of $v$ | First-order Taylor |
| 7 | Polar form: $u_r = \dfrac{1}{r}v_\theta,\; v_r = -\dfrac{1}{r}u_\theta$ | CR in polar coords | $z = r e^{i\theta}$ |
| 8 | $u_{xx} + u_{yy} = 0$ | Laplace's equation | $u$ harmonic (CR + $C^2$) |
| 9 | $v_{xx} + v_{yy} = 0$ | Laplace's equation | $v$ harmonic (CR + $C^2$) |
| 10 | $J = u_x v_y - u_y v_x = u_x^{\,2} + v_x^{\,2} = \vert f'(z)\vert^{2}$ | Jacobian determinant | Valid under CR |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2-Mark Booster:** The relation $f'(z) = u_x + i v_x = v_y - i u_y$ is the **single most-asked identity**. Memorise it cold.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of CR Equations |
|:--|:--|
| **Fluid Dynamics** | If $u$ is the velocity potential and $v$ is the stream function of an incompressible, irrotational 2D flow, the CR equations guarantee that the complex potential $\Phi = u + iv$ is analytic. |
| **Electromagnetic Field Theory** | Conjugate harmonic pairs $(u, v)$ model equipotential lines and field lines in 2D electrostatics. |
| **Aerospace Engineering** | Airfoil design (Joukowski transform) requires analytic maps $f(z)$ — CR is the gatekeeper. |
| **Signal Processing** | Holomorphic filters preserve phase and magnitude structure; CR conditions ensure causality. |
| **Cartography (Conformal Mapping)** | CR equations guarantee that small shapes are preserved (angles, but not sizes) — used in GPS projection software. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the CR Equations

**Statement to Prove:** If $f(z) = u(x, y) + i\,v(x, y)$ is differentiable at $z_0 = x_0 + iy_0$, then $u_x = v_y$ and $u_y = -v_x$ at $z_0$.

---

### Step 1 — Set up the difference quotient

By definition of the complex derivative:

$$
f'(z) = \lim_{\Delta z \to 0} \frac{f(z + \Delta z) - f(z)}{\Delta z}
$$

Let $\Delta z = \Delta x + i\,\Delta y$. Then $\Delta x, \Delta y \to 0$ independently. The increment in $f$ is:

$$
\Delta f = \left[u(x + \Delta x, y + \Delta y) - u(x, y)\right] + i \left[v(x + \Delta x, y + \Delta y) - v(x, y)\right]
$$

Define $\Delta u = u(x + \Delta x, y + \Delta y) - u(x, y)$ and $\Delta v = v(x + \Delta x, y + \Delta y) - v(x, y)$. Thus:

$$
\frac{\Delta f}{\Delta z} = \frac{\Delta u + i\,\Delta v}{\Delta x + i\,\Delta y}
$$

---

### Step 2 — Path-Independent Limit Argument

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The complex derivative $f'(z)$ **must be unique**, regardless of the path along which $\Delta z \to 0$. This forces the CR equations. We exploit two canonical paths.

**Path A: $\Delta z$ approaches along the real axis** — set $\Delta y = 0$, so $\Delta z = \Delta x \in \mathbb{R}$.

$$
f'(z) = \lim_{\Delta x \to 0} \frac{\Delta u + i\,\Delta v}{\Delta x}
$$

Since $u$ and $v$ are real functions of two real variables with partial derivatives, we have $\Delta u \approx u_x\,\Delta x$ and $\Delta v \approx v_x\,\Delta x$ for small $\Delta x$:

$$
f'(z) = \lim_{\Delta x \to 0}\left(\frac{u_x\,\Delta x}{\Delta x} + i\,\frac{v_x\,\Delta x}{\Delta x}\right) = u_x + i\,v_x
$$

**Path B: $\Delta z$ approaches along the imaginary axis** — set $\Delta x = 0$, so $\Delta z = i\,\Delta y$.

$$
f'(z) = \lim_{\Delta y \to 0} \frac{\Delta u + i\,\Delta v}{i\,\Delta y}
$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $-i$ (since $1/i = -i$):

$$
f'(z) = \lim_{\Delta y \to 0} \frac{-i(\Delta u + i\,\Delta v)}{\Delta y} = \lim_{\Delta y \to 0}\left(\frac{\Delta v}{\Delta y} - i\,\frac{\Delta u}{\Delta y}\right)
$$

Approximating $\Delta v \approx v_y\,\Delta y$ and $\Delta u \approx u_y\,\Delta y$:

$$
f'(z) = v_y - i\,u_y
$$

---

### Step 3 — Equate the two expressions for $f'(z)$

$$
u_x + i\,v_x = v_y - i\,u_y
$$

Matching real parts: $\quad u_x = v_y \quad$ (CR Equation 1)
Matching imaginary parts: $\quad v_x = -u_y \quad$ (CR Equation 2)

> [!NOTE]
> $\therefore$ The CR equations are **necessary** for differentiability of $f$ at $z_0$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — KTU Standard Problem

**Problem:** Show that $f(z) = e^z \cos y$ is analytic and find $f'(z)$.

**Solution:** Given $f(z) = e^z \cos y$. Since $z = x + iy$, we have $e^z = e^{x+iy} = e^x(\cos y + i\sin y)$. So:

$$
f(z) = e^x \cos y \cos y + i\,e^x \cos y \sin y
$$

Therefore:

$$
u(x, y) = e^x \cos^2 y, \qquad v(x, y) = e^x \sin y \cos y
$$

**Step 1 — Compute the partials:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_x &= e^x \cos^2 y \\
u_y &= e^x \cdot 2\cos y \cdot (-\sin y) = -2e^x \sin y \cos y \\
v_x &= e^x \sin y \cos y \\
v_y &= e^x(\cos^2 y - \sin^2 y) = e^x \cos 2y
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Verify CR:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_x &= e^x \cos^2 y \\
v_y &= e^x \cos 2y = e^x(\cos^2 y - \sin^2 y)
\end{aligned}
$$

These are **not equal** in general, hence $f(z) = e^z \cos y$ is **not analytic** (it doesn't even satisfy CR).

**Correct Example:** $f(z) = e^z = e^x \cos y + i\,e^x \sin y$, so $u = e^x \cos y$, $v = e^x \sin y$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_x &= e^x \cos y, & u_y &= -e^x \sin y \\
v_x &= e^x \sin y, & v_y &= e^x \cos y
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verify CR:** $u_x = e^x \cos y = v_y$ ✓ and $u_y = -e^x \sin y = -v_x$ ✓. Both equations hold for all $(x, y)$.

**Compute $f'(z)$:**

$$
f'(z) = u_x + i\,v_x = e^x \cos y + i\,e^x \sin y = e^x(\cos y + i\sin y) = e^z
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity check passes:** $f'(z) = e^z$, exactly as expected from the real-variable chain rule for $e^z$.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — "Is $f$ Analytic?"

| Step | Question | Action if "No" |
|:-:|:--|:--|
| 1 | Can you write $f$ in the form $u + iv$? | Convert using $z = x+iy$, $\bar z = x - iy$ |
| 2 | Do $u_x, u_y, v_x, v_y$ exist and are continuous? | If not, differentiability is in question |
| 3 | Does $u_x = v_y$ hold? | If not, **not analytic** |
| 4 | Does $u_y = -v_x$ hold? | If not, **not analytic** |
| 5 | If both hold everywhere in a domain, $f$ is analytic | Compute $f'(z) = u_x + i\,v_x$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Satisfying CR at a *single point* does **not** make $f$ analytic there. CR must hold in an open neighbourhood, with continuous partials. Example: $f(z) = \bar z / z$ for $z \ne 0$ extended by $f(0) = 0$ satisfies CR at the origin but is **not differentiable** there.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Logical Flow of the Cauchy–Riemann Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    B{"Identify u(x,y) and v(x,y)?"}:::decision

    E{"Are Partial Derivatives Continuous?"}:::decision

    H["Check CR Equation 2: u_y = -v_x"]:::verify

    K["f is Analytic in Domain D"]:::success

    M["Output: f-prime z and Domain"]:::success

    A --> B
    B -- Yes --> C
    B -- No --> J
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E -- No --> F

    G -- Holds --> H

    H -- Fails --> J

    I -- No --> J
    K --> L
    L --> M

```

## 4.2 Block Architecture — Equivalence of CR and Path-Independence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Complex Derivative Definition"]:::startNode
    B["Path A: Real-axis approach"]:::process
    C["f-prime z = u_x + i v_x"]:::formula
    D["Path B: Imaginary-axis approach"]:::process
    E["f-prime z = v_y - i u_y"]:::formula
    F["Equate Real Parts"]:::process
    G["u_x = v_y"]:::cr
    H["Equate Imaginary Parts"]:::process
    I["u_y = -v_x"]:::cr
    J["Cauchy-Riemann System"]:::success

    A --> B
    A --> D
    B --> C
    D --> E
    C --> F
    C --> H
    E --> F
    E --> H
    F --> G
    H --> I
    G --> J
    I --> J
```
